title: Operation Datafall
brief: Use Chen's harvested password to log into the Orion jump server. Pull her home directory and shared drives with rsync, then grep through the dump to find a private SSH key, a database connection string, and a colleague's contact for the next operation.

debrief.success: Three artifacts recovered: SSH key, DB DSN, and a peer contact. That key opens the next door — Vasquez's bastion host. Stand by for the next contract.
debrief.fail: Session burned. Orion's SOC flagged your credential reuse — they'll rotate Chen's password and lock down the jump host. Retry with a lighter touch.

emails.subject: Contract update — Datafall (credential chain)
emails.body:
Operator—

The password you harvested from Chen is still live. Orion has no MFA on their jump host — classic gap.

Log in as m.chen, then use rsync to pull her shares. Grep the dump for secrets: SSH keys, config files with database strings, contact lists.

What is rsync? It synchronizes files between hosts over SSH — think cp, but for remote trees. What is grep? It searches file contents for patterns — defenders use it to find leaked secrets; you will too.

— Sable

emails.subject: Rules of engagement — Datafall
emails.body:
Operator—

This op uses a stolen credential. You are cleared for read-only access.

- Do not modify files on the target.
- Exfil only what is listed in the objective.
- If SOC correlates the login with the phish, spoof or laylow before retrying.

— Sable, Handler

emails.subject: Jump server topology (unofficial)
emails.body:
Unofficial routing: local → corp-jump → file-share.

Chen's credential gets you past the jump host SSH. The NAS is on the same subnet — no additional auth required after the jump.

Hint: rsync is quieter than reading files one by one.

steps.title: Logged in as Chen
steps.text: You're on the jump server. Use rsync file-share:/shares/m.chen /dump to pull the share — it's quieter than reading files one by one.

steps.title: Share accessible
steps.text: Files are on the share. Use grep to search for secrets: try grep id_rsa, grep DATABASE_URL, or grep @ to find keys, DSN strings, and contacts.

objective.summary: Exfiltrate Chen's private SSH key from the file share.

services.name: ssh
services.vulnRef: Credential stuffing — reused password from phished account; no MFA on jump host

files.content:
*** ORION LOGISTICS — INTERNAL JUMP HOST ***
Authorized users only. All sessions are logged.
Report suspicious activity to soc@orion.internal.

rsync -avz file-share:/shares/m.chen /tmp/backup
grep -r 'DATABASE' /tmp/backup/projects/
ssh -i ~/.ssh/id_rsa j.vasquez@nexus-core.internal
ls -la /shares/m.chen/.ssh/

services.name: ssh
services.vulnRef: CVE-2024-6387 (OpenSSH regreSSHion) — internal NAS; no additional auth after jump

files.content:

[FICTION — this key does not unlock any real system]

artifact.description: Chen's private SSH key — unlocks bastion hosts she connected to, including j.vasquez@nexus-core.internal.

files.content:
# Orion ops environment — DO NOT COMMIT
APP_ENV=production
DATABASE_URL=postgres://svc_ops:Orion_DB#7r@db-internal:5432/ops_prod
REDIS_URL=redis://cache-internal:6379/0
SECRET_KEY=rotating-token-placeholder

artifact.description: Production database connection string for Orion ops DB — credentials for svc_ops role on db-internal.

files.content:
Team contacts (internal only)
- m.chen@corp.orion.net (me)
- r.park@corp.orion.net (infra)
- j.vasquez@nexus-core.internal (external contractor — API integration)

peer contact: j.vasquez@nexus-core.internal

artifact.description: Vasquez's internal email address — useful for social engineering or identifying bastion hosts in the Nexus domain.

files.content:
Q1 review notes:
- API latency up 12% since migration
- Vasquez says Nexus bastion needs key rotation (hasn't happened)
- Park wants to move DB credentials out of .env files (good luck)
- Reminder: do NOT store SSH keys on the share drive (oops)
